title: Dog Bleeding From Nose (Epistaxis): Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's nose bleeding? Learn why epistaxis is a high-priority emergency and how to document it for your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Epistaxis?

Epistaxis is the clinical term for bleeding from the nose. In dogs, this can appear as a slow drip, a steady stream, or even blood being sprayed when the dog sneezes. It may occur in one nostril (unilateral) or both (bilateral) and can be caused by various factors ranging from local trauma to systemic clotting issues.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Any instance of a bloody nose in a dog should be treated with significant concern. While a minor scrape might cause a small amount of blood, internal causes like rodenticide (rat poison) ingestion, blood clotting disorders, or severe infections are life-threatening. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if the bleeding is heavy, does not stop within five minutes, or if your dog appears weak or has pale gums.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo or video of the bleeding before you head to the clinic can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. A visual record allows the vet to see the volume of blood lost, the specific location of the flow, and the color of the blood—all of which provide clues to the underlying cause while your pet is being stabilized.

faqs.question: What should I do to stop the bleeding?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm as possible to keep their blood pressure low. You can gently apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose if your dog allows it, but do not stick anything inside the nostrils.

faqs.question: Could my dog have eaten something toxic?
faqs.answer: Yes, one common cause of sudden nosebleeds is the ingestion of anticoagulant rat poisons. If there is any chance your dog accessed pesticides, bring the packaging with you to the vet.

faqs.question: What if my dog is also sneezing?
faqs.answer: Sneezing often accompanies epistaxis. It can indicate a foreign object, like a grass awn, stuck in the nasal passage, or an upper respiratory infection that has damaged the nasal lining.
